title: Topic 46: Loss Chasing: The Dangerous Psychology of Winning It Back
content:
# Topic 46: Loss Chasing: The Dangerous Psychology of Winning It Back

## Abstract

This paper delves into the curious human phenomenon of “loss chasing,” a behavior that turns the simple act of losing into the art of catastrophic financial self-immolation. We explore the psychological underpinnings of why, when faced with a loss, some individuals choose not to gracefully retreat, but to double down, armed with a shovel of misplaced optimism and a steadfast refusal to cut their losses. Through a synthesis of cognitive science, behavioral economics, and a healthy dose of schadenfreude, we examine the irrational decision-making that transforms a minor setback into a life-altering disaster. This paper will not make you better at gambling, but it will provide a well-researched and morbidly entertaining explanation for why you, and so many others, are so terrible at it. We will analyze the cognitive biases that make us susceptible to chasing losses, from the sunk cost fallacy to the illusion of control, and present a case study that serves as a monument to the dangers of a surfeit of money and a deficit of sense. In short, this is an academic exploration of the human tendency to dig a hole, and then, upon realizing the depth of the hole, to decide that the only logical next step is to rent a backhoe.

## Introduction

It begins, as so many tragedies do, with a vending machine. You insert your dollar, press the button for a bag of chips, and watch in horror as the metal coil, a tiny, merciless tyrant, rotates just enough to tease, but not to release. The chips hang there, a salty, foil-packaged Tantalus, mocking you from their plastic prison. You have lost a dollar. A rational actor, the *homo economicus* of academic fantasy, would walk away. The potential reward is not worth the additional risk. But you are not a rational actor. You are a human being, and you have been wronged by a machine. So you put in another dollar. And perhaps another. Before you know it, you have spent five dollars on a one-dollar bag of chips, and you are shaking the machine, screaming, “I will win, you son of a biscuit!”

This, in a nutshell, is loss chasing. It is the irrational, often compulsive, urge to recoup a loss by taking on even greater risk. It is a behavior that is most famously associated with gambling, but as our vending machine melodrama illustrates, it is a deeply ingrained human tendency. It is the stock trader who averages down on a failing stock, the poker player who goes on “tilt” after a bad beat, and the art collector who, having been duped by a forgery, buys another, even more dubious, piece from the same dealer in a desperate attempt to “make it back.”

This paper will explore the dangerous and often darkly comical psychology of loss chasing. We will examine the cognitive biases and emotional dysfunctions that lead us down this path of self-destruction. We will review the academic literature on the subject, from the foundational work of Kahneman and Tversky to more recent research on the neurological correlates of this peculiar behavior. We will also delve into the real-world consequences of loss chasing, with a particular focus on the story of Terrance Watanabe, a man who turned losing into a veritable art form, to the tune of over $200 million. Ultimately, this paper aims to provide an insightful, and hopefully entertaining, look at why human beings are so stubbornly committed to the idea that the best way to get out of a hole is to keep digging.

## Literature Review

To understand the peculiar dance of loss chasing, one must first consult the academic choreographers who have mapped out its steps. The intellectual bedrock of our understanding of loss chasing can be traced back to the groundbreaking work of Daniel Kahneman and Amos Tversky, who in their 1979 paper on Prospect Theory, first articulated the concept of “loss aversion.” [1] The theory, which is as elegant as it is depressing, posits that for the average human, the psychological pain of losing is approximately twice as powerful as the pleasure of winning. In other words, we are not the rational, calculating machines that classical economics would have us believe. We are, in fact, a bundle of emotional and cognitive biases, and the fear of losing looms large in our decision-making. This aversion to loss is the fertile soil in which the seeds of loss chasing are sown.

Building on this foundation is the equally pervasive “sunk cost fallacy,” the misguided belief that one should continue a course of action because of the resources already invested, regardless of the future prospects. It is the reason we finish a terrible movie, force down a mediocre meal, and, of course, throw good money after bad. The gambler, having already lost a significant sum, feels compelled to continue, not because they believe they have a good chance of winning, but because they cannot bear the thought of their previous losses being “for nothing.” The money is already gone, a “sunk cost,” but the gambler’s mind, in its infinite capacity for self-deception, believes that with just one more bet, it can all be reclaimed.

More recent research has added another layer of complexity to our understanding of loss chasing, suggesting that it may not just be a matter of cognitive bias, but also of emotional dysfunction. The work of Bibby and Ferguson on “alexithymia” has been particularly illuminating in this regard. [2] Alexithymia, a term that sounds like it should be the name of a forgotten Greek goddess but is in fact a personality trait characterized by an inability to identify and describe emotions, has been found to be a significant predictor of loss-chasing behavior. Individuals high in alexithymia, it seems, have difficulty processing the emotional consequences of their losses. They feel the sting of a loss, but they are unable to properly regulate the emotional response, leading to a state of impulsive, and often destructive, decision-making. The brain, it turns out, is not a fan of emotional ambiguity, and when it cannot make sense of its own feelings, it is prone to making very bad choices.

Finally, the neurocognitive perspective offers a glimpse into the brain on tilt. Studies have shown that loss chasing is associated with altered activity in the prefrontal cortex and the anterior cingulate cortex, regions of the brain that are responsible for executive functions like inhibitory control and emotional regulation. [3] In essence, when a gambler is chasing losses, the part of their brain that is supposed to be the responsible adult in the room has gone on a smoke break, leaving the impulsive, thrill-seeking toddler in charge. This convergence of cognitive biases, emotional dysregulation, and neurological dysfunction creates a perfect storm of irrationality, a storm that can, and often does, lead to financial ruin.

## Analysis

Having reviewed the scholarly literature, which, let's be honest, is a bit like reading the minutes of a very long and depressing meeting, we can now turn our attention to the more visceral, and frankly, more interesting, aspects of loss chasing. What is it that actually happens in the mind of a gambler as they slide from a state of mild disappointment to one of full-blown, kamikaze-style self-destruction? The answer, it seems, lies in a potent cocktail of cognitive distortions, emotional hijinks, and a fundamental misunderstanding of the laws of probability.

One of the key ingredients in this toxic brew is the “near-miss” effect. The slot machine that stops with the winning symbol just one position out of alignment, the roulette ball that settles in the number next to yours, the horse that leads for the entire race only to be pipped at the post – these are not just losses, they are invitations. They are the universe whispering in your ear, “You were so close! Try again!” This illusion of being on the verge of a win is a powerful motivator, and it is a key driver of loss-chasing behavior. The gambler, convinced that they have “cracked the code” or are “due for a win,” continues to bet, not realizing that each spin, each roll, each race, is an independent event, and the universe, far from whispering encouragement, is in fact, completely indifferent to their fate.

Then there is the “illusion of control,” the gambler’s unshakable belief that they can, through some combination of skill, ritual, and sheer force of will, influence the outcome of a random event. This is the craps player who believes that a certain way of throwing the dice will produce a favorable result, the blackjack player who is convinced that their “system” will beat the house, and the lottery player who uses their children’s birthdays as their lucky numbers. This belief in one’s own agency in the face of randomness is a powerful antidote to the feelings of helplessness that can accompany a string of losses. It is also, of course, a complete and utter delusion. The dice, the cards, and the lottery balls are not impressed by your system, your ritual, or your children’s birthdays. They are, and always will be, slaves to the laws of probability.

But perhaps the most insidious aspect of loss chasing is its role as a form of escapism. For many, the act of gambling is not about winning money, but about escaping from the pressures and anxieties of everyday life. The casino, with its flashing lights, its constant noise, and its free-flowing alcohol, is a world unto itself, a world where the rules of normal life do not apply. In this world, the gambler is not a person with a mortgage, a dead-end job, and a nagging sense of existential dread. They are a player, a high roller, a person of action. And when the losses start to mount, the gambler is faced with a choice: they can either stop playing and return to the dreary reality of their life, or they can continue to gamble, and in doing so, prolong the escape. For many, the choice is clear. They will continue to chase their losses, not because they believe they can win, but because they cannot bear the thought of stopping.

## Real-World Examples

To truly appreciate the destructive power of loss chasing, we must move beyond the realm of theory and into the messy, often tragic, world of real life. And in the pantheon of loss chasers, there is one name that stands above all others, a man who did not just chase his losses, but pursued them with the single-minded determination of a heat-seeking missile: Terrance Watanabe.

Watanabe, a successful businessman who had inherited and grown a multi-million dollar party-favor business, was, by all accounts, a man who knew how to make money. Unfortunately, he was even better at losing it. After selling his company in 2000, Watanabe embarked on a gambling career that can only be described as epic. In a single year, 2007, he managed to lose an astonishing $127 million at Caesars Palace and The Rio in Las Vegas, having wagered a total of $825 million. His total losses are estimated to be in the neighborhood of $204 million, a sum that is difficult to comprehend, let alone lose. [4]

Watanabe’s story is a masterclass in the psychology of loss chasing. He was a man who was not just chasing his losses, but was being actively encouraged to do so by the very institutions that were taking his money. Caesars Entertainment, it was later revealed, had created a special “Chairman” tier in their loyalty program just for him, plying him with perks that included $500,000 in gift shop credit, tickets to see the Rolling Stones, and, most tellingly, a 15% rebate on his losses. They also, it was alleged, allowed him to gamble while heavily intoxicated, a practice that earned them a slap-on-the-wrist fine of $225,000 from the New Jersey Gaming Commission. [5] Watanabe, for his part, claimed that the casino had plied him with alcohol and prescription drugs, turning him into a “zombie-like” state. The truth, as is so often the case, is likely somewhere in the middle. But what is clear is that Watanabe was a man in the grip of a powerful and destructive compulsion, a compulsion that was being actively enabled by those who stood to profit from it.

But one does not need to be a multi-millionaire to fall victim to the siren song of loss chasing. The same psychological forces that led Watanabe to financial ruin are at play in the lives of ordinary people every day. Consider the amateur stock trader, who, having seen their investment in a “sure thing” tech stock plummet, decides to “average down,” buying more shares at a lower price in the hopes of breaking even. Or the video gamer, who, having lost a valuable in-game item, spends the entire night grinding away in a desperate attempt to win it back. Or, to return to our opening example, the person who, having lost a dollar in a vending machine, proceeds to spend another five in a futile attempt to retrieve their prize. In each of these cases, the logic is the same: the loss is not a signal to stop, but a challenge to be overcome, a wrong to be righted. And in each of these cases, the outcome is almost always the same: the hole just gets deeper.

## Discussion

The psychology of loss chasing, while most dramatically illustrated in the high-stakes world of gambling, is not confined to the casino floor. The same cognitive biases and emotional vulnerabilities that drive the gambler to ruin can be seen in a wide range of consumer behaviors. In a world that is increasingly designed to capture and hold our attention, and to separate us from our money, understanding the psychology of loss chasing is more important than ever.

Consider the world of retail. The “buy one, get one free” offer, the limited-time discount, the loyalty program that promises rewards for continued spending – these are all, in their own way, forms of loss chasing. The consumer, having already committed to a purchase, is presented with the opportunity to “win back” some of their expenditure, either in the form of a free product, a discount, or a future reward. The logic is seductive: the more you spend, the more you “save.” But as with gambling, the house always wins. The consumer, lured by the promise of a deal, often ends up spending more than they had originally intended, chasing a “win” that is, in reality, a carefully constructed illusion.

Then there is the world of digital media and subscription services. The “freemium” model, in which a basic service is offered for free, with additional features available for a fee, is a masterclass in the art of the sunk cost fallacy. The user, having already invested their time and energy in learning and using the free version of the service, is more likely to upgrade to the paid version, not because they necessarily need the additional features, but because they do not want their initial investment to be “wasted.” Similarly, the subscription service that is difficult to cancel, or that offers a discount for a long-term commitment, is playing on our aversion to loss. We are reluctant to cancel a service that we have already paid for, even if we are not using it, because to do so would be to admit that our initial investment was a mistake.

Even our relationship with brands can be seen through the lens of loss chasing. The consumer who has always bought a certain brand of car, or phone, or coffee, may continue to do so, even when a better or cheaper alternative becomes available. This brand loyalty is often driven by a sense of sunk cost. The consumer has invested not just their money, but also their identity, in the brand. To switch to a different brand would be to admit that their previous choices were wrong, a loss that they are not willing to accept. And so they continue to buy the same product, chasing the validation of their past decisions, even as the world changes around them.

## Conclusion

In the grand casino of life, we are all, to some extent, gamblers. We wager our time, our money, and our emotional energy in the hopes of a favorable return. And, inevitably, we all experience loss. The question is not whether we will lose, but how we will respond when we do. Will we, like the rational actor of economic theory, cut our losses and walk away? Or will we, like the hapless protagonist of our story, double down, and chase our losses into the abyss?

As we have seen, the path of the loss chaser is a well-trodden one. It is a path that is paved with cognitive biases, emotional dysregulation, and a fundamental misunderstanding of the laws of probability. It is a path that is as old as humanity itself, and as new as the latest mobile game. And it is a path that, for many, leads to financial ruin, emotional distress, and a profound sense of regret.

But there is hope. By understanding the psychology of loss chasing, we can begin to recognize it in ourselves and in others. We can learn to identify the cognitive distortions that lead us astray, and to develop the emotional regulation skills that are necessary to resist the siren song of the “big win.” We can, in short, learn to be better gamblers. Not in the sense of winning more often, but in the sense of losing more wisely. And in a world that is full of uncertainty and risk, that may be the most valuable skill of all.
references:
[1] Kahneman, D., & Tversky, A. (1979). Prospect Theory: An Analysis of Decision under Risk. *Econometrica*, *47*(2), 263–291. https://doi.org/10.2307/1914185

[2] Bibby, P. A., & Ferguson, E. (2016). Loss-Chasing, Alexithymia, and Impulsivity in a Gambling Task. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *7*, 3. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2016.00003

[3] Zhang, K., & Clark, L. (2020). Loss-chasing in gambling behaviour: neurocognitive and behavioural economic perspectives. *Current Opinion in Behavioral Sciences*, *31*, 28-33.

[4] Berzon, A. (2009, December 5). The Gambler Who Blew $127 Million. *The Wall Street Journal*. Retrieved from https://www.wsj.com/articles/SB125997538354592083

[5] Pempus, B. (2013, March 21). Caesars Fined $225K Over Not Kicking Out Belligerent High-Roller On Historic Losing Streak. *Card Player*. Retrieved from https://www.cardplayer.com/poker-news/15031-caesars-fined-225-000-over-not-kicking-out-belligerent-high-roller-on-historic-losing-streak
